videos.title: $3.3M Fine for Alleged Egg Price Fixing Is a Tiny Fraction of Suppliers’ Record Profits
Egg producers reach settlement over price-fixing complaint
Egg producers will pay $3.3M and donate 53M eggs to settle price-fixing claims
Egg Producers Settle DOJ Lawsuit for Price Fixing

claim_verification.claim: The DOJ and 17 state attorneys general filed a civil antitrust lawsuit against three of the nation's largest egg producers in 2026.
The three companies named in the lawsuit are Cal-Maine Foods, Versova, and Hickman's Egg Ranch.
The producers allegedly coordinated their bidding on the Egg Clearinghouse Inc. platform to artificially inflate prices.
The manipulation targeted Urner Barry, a market reporting company whose benchmark prices are used in contracts throughout the egg industry.
The alleged price coordination took place between June 2022 and March 2025.
The three companies agreed to collectively pay $3.3 million to the participating states.
The $3.3 million fine was described as a tiny fraction of the profits made during the price-fixing period.
The producers agreed to donate 53 million eggs to food banks and nonprofit organizations as part of the settlement.
None of the three companies admitted any wrongdoing or liability as part of the settlement.
The settlement includes a five-year compliance term requiring antitrust compliance programs.
The companies are required to appoint a DOJ-approved compliance officer under the settlement terms.
Cal-Maine Foods is the largest producer and distributor of fresh shell eggs in the United States.
Cal-Maine Foods reported net income of approximately $758 million in fiscal year 2023 during the period of record egg prices.
Cal-Maine Foods reported record net income of approximately $1.2 billion in fiscal year 2025.
The companies claimed that price surges were driven by avian influenza outbreaks, supply disruptions, and inflation, not coordination.
Egg producers reported record profits during the price-fixing period.
